← Course Index

Union Types & State

Mastering dynamic logic in TypeScript.

Today we will build a guessing game to explore how TypeScript handles changing data and multiple types.

Agenda

  • Configuration vs. Mutable State
  • Union Types (Pipe Operator)
  • Type Guards & Narrowing
  • Block Scoping Best Practices
  • Modern ESM Project Setup
We will cover everything from basic variable declaration to advanced project configuration.

Config vs. State

  • Use const for fixed values
  • Target numbers remain immutable
  • Use let for loop flags
  • State changes during execution
Distinguishing between static configuration and dynamic state is fundamental to predictable application logic.

Union Types

  • Handle unpredictable user input
  • Use the pipe operator (|)
  • Allows multiple specific types
  • Example: number | string
In our game, a user might enter a number to guess or a string command like 'quit'.

Union Type Example

let playerGuess: number | string;

if (rawInput === 'quit') {
    playerGuess = 'quit';
} else {
    playerGuess = parseFloat(rawInput);
}
This informs the compiler that the variable is valid in either state, preventing early errors.

The Type Guard

  • Narrow types for specific logic
  • Use the typeof operator
  • Enables math on union types
  • Prevents compiler errors
TypeScript won't let you do math on a 'string | number' until you prove it is a number.

Implementing Guards

if (typeof playerGuess === 'number') {
    if (isNaN(playerGuess)) {
        console.log("Invalid number");
    } else {
        const diff = Math.abs(playerGuess - secret);
    }
}
Inside the if-block, the compiler treats the variable strictly as a number.

The NaN Gotcha

  • parseFloat returns number type
  • Result can still be NaN
  • Always use isNaN() check
  • Ensures true numeric validation
Even if the type is 'number', NaN can break your logic, so runtime checks are still necessary.

Block Scoping

  • Declare variables inside logic blocks
  • Prevents naming conflicts
  • Keeps global scope clean
  • Use let and const locally
Declaring temporary variables like 'difference' inside specific blocks improves memory and readability.

Project Setup

  • Avoid bloated tsc --init
  • Use manual tsconfig.json
  • Transition to modern ESM
  • Set type: module in package
Modern TypeScript development favors ECMAScript Modules over the older CommonJS standard.

ESM Configuration

{
  "name": "guessing-game",
  "type": "module",
  "devDependencies": {
    "typescript": "^5.0.0"
  }
}
Setting type to module in package.json is essential for modern Node.js environments.

TSConfig Strategy

  • Keep configuration slim
  • Target modern ES versions
  • Enable strict type checking
  • Maintain clean project structure
A slimmed-down config is often easier for beginners to manage than the default generated file.

Summary

  • Const for config, let for state
  • Unions allow flexible input types
  • Type guards enable safe math
  • ESM is the modern standard
Remember: always narrow your types before performing operations to keep TypeScript happy.
